briefing.opening: Hello everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for June 1, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Worsening Climate Crisis, A Global Warning
sections.content: News released today once again underscores the severity of the deepening global climate crisis. In India, Pakistan, and Europe, deadly heatwaves reaching 40 degrees Celsius are raging even outside of peak summer, causing casualties and changes in animal behavior [5, 6, 17, 30]. Notably, with the possibility of global temperatures breaking all-time records within the next five years [7, 20], countries are facing increased budgetary burdens for disaster prevention related to climate change, such as managing large-scale wildfire damage and preventing livestock losses [3, 28, 179]. Domestically, livelihood support funds are scheduled to be disbursed to farmers who suffered agricultural damage due to climate change last year [1, 159], and with heavy rain forecasted for Jeju and the southern coast starting in June [160], Prime Minister Kim emphasized [16] that the climate crisis is no longer a distant future concern but an immediate challenge. In this context, the stagnation or delay of amendments to the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act in the National Assembly [12, 33, 35, 158, 162] is drawing criticism for insufficient policy responses compared to the perceived reality of a climate catastrophe.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Impacts and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Climate change is already exerting widespread impacts across domestic ecosystems and industries, with active adaptation efforts underway. Due to climate change, the domestic fruit cultivation map is shifting, with Hongcheon emerging as a new major apple producing region [4, 11], thereby reshaping the agricultural landscape. In response, the Rural Development Administration has initiated the development of cabbage varieties resilient to heatwaves and droughts [14, 29], and Pyeongtaek City is selecting rice varieties suitable for climate change [24], accelerating climate change adaptation efforts in the agricultural sector. Furthermore, Jeonbuk Self-Governing Province has activated an emergency response headquarters for summer forest pests and diseases caused by climate change [9], taking proactive steps to protect ecosystems. Korea Water Resources Corporation (K-water) has also signed a digital water management agreement with Luxembourg to strengthen its climate change response capabilities [10]. Seongdong-gu is demonstrating successful regional adaptation efforts, having received an excellent evaluation in the 2025 carbon neutrality and climate crisis adaptation measures implementation review [100].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating the Transition to a Carbon-Neutral Society
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts to transition to a carbon-neutral society are expanding, centered on citizen participation and education. Korea Food for the Hungry International (KFHI) is hosting a 'Climate Change Response Project' competition for youth to discover innovative ideas from the future generation [2, 8, 15, 19, 26, 27, 32]. Various local governments, including Yeoncheon-gun and Daedeok-gu, are fostering environmental awareness by operating 'visiting' carbon neutrality education and children's experience programs [50, 52, 57, 58, 79, 84, 86, 94]. Cities like Siheung, Namyangju, and Yongin are fostering a citizen-led carbon neutrality practice culture through initiatives such as launching climate citizen networks, training citizen environmental interpreters, and operating environmental education weeks [36, 37, 38, 39, 41, 42, 59, 61, 64, 71, 73, 77, 81, 92, 96, 99, 101]. Furthermore, cities like Seogwipo and Cheongju are encouraging carbon neutrality practices in daily life through incentives for the Carbon Neutrality Point System and promoting the citizen challenge 'Today is Green' [48, 51, 54, 62, 69, 70, 80, 88, 93, 95]. Dr. Hwang Byung-dae is exploring new possibilities by suggesting that agriculture can be a solution to the climate crisis through the publication of 'Carbon Regenerative Agriculture' [31, 43].

sections.title: 🌱 Expanding Renewable Energy, A Leap Towards Future Energy
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy for a sustainable future is accelerating both domestically and internationally. The Korea Environment Corporation (K-ECO) is leading public institutions in practicing carbon neutrality by declaring 'RE100,' committing to convert 100% of its electricity consumption to renewable energy by 2030 [45, 111, 114, 118, 137]. Taihan Electric Wire is expanding its presence in the renewable energy grid connection market by securing a 50 billion KRW Haenam solar power grid project [102, 104, 107, 117, 119, 120, 125, 127, 128, 131, 139, 140, 141, 142, 143, 148, 149, 152, 153, 154, 156]. Bright Energy Partners (BEP) and Hyundai Engineering & Construction are also responding to RE100 demand through a 1.6 GW solar PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) supply cooperation [113, 115, 121, 122, 132, 151, 157]. The government has begun researching the potential use of river areas as new sites for solar power generation [124], and 25 types of AI public data, including renewable energy potential, have been opened [105, 130, 134, 150], laying the groundwork for efficient renewable energy utilization. However, challenges such as the need to strengthen safety management for renewable energy facilities [116] and questions regarding the achievement of national renewable energy targets [133] also persist.

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Response and New Discussions
sections.content: The international community continues discussions and research on various aspects of climate change. UN experts welcome a General Assembly resolution affirming states' obligations to combat climate change [168], and in-depth reflections are also taking place on legal and ethical issues, such as human rights to a healthy environment and the compatibility of deep-sea mining [165]. In the scientific field, diverse approaches are being attempted, including uncovering climate change information through Amazon tree rings [172] and strengthening research on climate change and immune health through fellowships [169]. Meanwhile, controversy is arising as the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) proposes repealing climate change disclosure regulations [163, 170], and practical barriers to policy implementation are also emerging, such as in Ireland where citizens believe in climate change but are reluctant to pay additional costs [177]. Research is also underway on whether virtual reality can increase public interest in climate change [171], with new attempts being explored to raise awareness and change behavior regarding climate change.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the severity of the climate crisis, alongside the convergence of diverse domestic and international efforts to overcome it. We must continue to foster change for the future through sustained attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: On May 21, 2024, the final public hearing for a climate constitutional petition, questioning whether the South Korean government's response to climate change aligns with the constitution, took place in front of the Constitutional Court in Jongno-gu, Seoul. Citizens, including petitioners from the 'Baby Climate Lawsuit,' held a press conference advocating for a declaration of unconstitutionality. This comes as the amendment to the 'Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth for Climate Crisis Response,' which outlines the pace of carbon reduction for achieving carbon neutrality by 2050, faces delays.

summary.phenomenon: On May 21, 2024, the final public hearing for a climate constitutional petition, questioning whether the South Korean government's response to climate change aligns with the constitution, took place in front of the Constitutional Court in Jongno-gu, Seoul. Related to this, the amendment to the 'Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth for Climate Crisis Response,' which specifies the pace of carbon reduction to achieve carbon neutrality by 2050, is currently being delayed.

summary.phenomenon: In 2025, 'devastating' megafires occurred in wealthier regions worldwide, including California, Canada, Europe, and South Korea, causing damage to lives, homes, and jobs. Globally, 335 million hectares were burned, marking the second-lowest figure since 2002.
summary.cause: Changes in farming practices in parts of Africa slowed the spread of wildfires, significantly contributing to the global reduction in burned area.
